lesson_title: Introduction to function parameters and arguments

cards.text: A {{c1::parameter}} is a variable declared in the function definition. An {{c2::argument}} is the actual value passed to the function.

cards.front: What is the difference between a parameter and an argument?
cards.back: A parameter is the variable in the function declaration. An argument is the actual value passed when calling the function.

cards.text: Parameters are defined inside the {{c1::parentheses}} after the function name, with syntax: {{c2::type name}}

cards.question: What does this program output?
cards.code:
#include <iostream>

void printValue(int x) {
std::cout << x;
}

int main() {
printValue(5);
return 0;
}
cards.explanation: The function printValue takes parameter x. When called with argument 5, x is set to 5 and printed.

cards.text: Multiple parameters are separated by {{c1::commas}}. Example: void func(int x, {{c2::double y}}, {{c3::bool z}})
When a function is called, parameters are {{c1::created}} and {{c2::initialized}} with the values of the corresponding arguments. They act as {{c3::local variables}} inside the function.

cards.question: What does this function return?
cards.code:
int add(int x, int y) {
return x + y;
}

// Called as: add(3, 4)
cards.explanation: Parameters x and y are initialized with arguments 3 and 4. The function returns 3 + 4 = 7.

cards.question: What's wrong with this function call?
cards.code:
void printValues(int x, int y) { }

int main() {
printValues(5); // Error!
return 0;
}
cards.answer: Error: Wrong number of arguments. printValues expects 2 arguments but only 1 was provided.
cards.explanation: The number of arguments must match the number of parameters. Missing or extra arguments cause compilation errors.

cards.text: Arguments should match parameter {{c1::types}}. If types differ, {{c2::implicit conversion}} may occur if possible, otherwise a {{c3::compilation error}} results.
When arguments are passed to parameters, they are passed by {{c1::value}} (copied) by default in C++.

cards.explanation:
Parameters allow functions to be flexible and reusable:
  • Same function can work with different values
  • Enables code reuse without duplication
  • Makes functions more general-purpose
  • Allows functions to operate on data provided by caller

cards.question: How many parameters and arguments are in this code?
cards.code:
void multiply(int a, int b, int c) { }

int main() {
multiply(2, 3, 4);
return 0;
}
cards.explanation: The function definition has 3 parameters. The function call provides 3 arguments that correspond to those parameters.

cards.text: A function can have {{c1::zero}} parameters, indicated by {{c2::empty parentheses}}: void func()

cards.back: The actual value passed to a function when it is called
